title: High-Performance Reconfigurable Accelerator With Lightweight and Energy-Efficient Architecture for PPG-Based Blood Pressure Estimation
authors: Tran, Diem and Nguyen, Gia  Phuc and Nguyen, Hai  Thanh
abstract: Non-invasive blood pressure estimation using photoplethysmography (PPG) signals has emerged as a promising approach for continuous health monitoring; however, achieving real-time and energy-efficient on-device inference remains challenging with conventional CPU/GPU-based platforms. Although FPGA-based accelerators provide a viable alternative, existing designs are often specialized for specific operators or models, leading to fragmented architectures and suboptimal resource utilization when supporting hybrid deep learning networks. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a reconfigurable CNN–LSTM accelerator implemented on an FPGA-based system-on-chip (SoC), featuring a unified hardware architecture that efficiently supports both convolutional and recurrent computations within a single framework. The design leverages a shared 16-processing-element (PE) array with a unified datapath to eliminate redundant modules and enable flexible layer-wise reconfiguration. Furthermore, a pipeline-optimized multiply–accumulate (MAC) structure combined with inter-PE parallelism enhances computational throughput, while a dataflow-aware memory optimization strategy significantly reduces on-chip memory usage and data movement overhead. The proposed accelerator is implemented on a Xilinx KV260 platform and evaluated using the MIMIC-III dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that the design achieves a throughput of 1.142 GOP/s with a hardware efficiency of 47.53 GOP/s/MeLUT, while requiring only 15.3K LUTs (24.0K eLUT), 17 DSPs, and 5 BRAMs. Operating at 150 MHz with a power consumption of 0.386 W, the proposed design provides a favorable balance between performance and resource cost compared to prior FPGA-based implementations. Despite its compact hardware footprint, the FPGA implementation maintains strong predictive performance, achieving mean absolute errors of 5.64 mmHg for systolic and 3.89 mmHg for diastolic blood pressure. Overall, the proposed architecture establishes an efficient design point that combines unified computation, reconfigurability, and high hardware efficiency, making it well-suited for real-time PPG-based blood pressure estimation in resource-constrained edge computing environments.
